speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape-- tape four of an interview on January 16, 1996 with Mr. Kurt Moses.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe if I would have told her the truth then, she probably wouldn't have survived. And luckily, she did survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went back first. I had a-- I was very fortunate. I went to Pilsen, Czechoslovakia. We got-- that was an American zone. And we got a flight back to France. And from France, I went back to Holland. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My mother's sister and her brother, who were hidden during the war, had come out of hiding and had started their business again. We had a butcher shop. So we found each other, and I moved in with them. And-- and then my sister came and my mother, and we moved in with them. Of course, when-- we had to tell my mother the truth when she came. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it never was the same again. She just couldn't cope with it. It was a very, very sad situation. There was nothing that meant anything to her. And we tried to convince her that my sister lied. And we need here, a she has to go on. And she stayed with us. And she did. And she came with us to America, too. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she just didn't want to go on. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] She died after one year in America. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] It was sad. I guess once she saw that we were set, we were able to make it ourselves, that she was no longer needed. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] I think she was 52 years old. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then life went on. And you can't live in the past all your life. When did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: January, 1947. And then I realized you can't live in the past. You have to go on. You have to start a new life for yourself. And you have to use the old medicine we used in Holland. When I said, sterk, you gotta be strong. And you put-- pull yourself together, and you try to be strong. And you go on, and you make a new life for yourself. And I was very fortunate because with my trade, I had no problem finding a job. I was-- the first place I went to I was hired. Especially you were a European and German cabinet-maker, you were well in demand. I remember I started off with $1 an hour. I was the richest man in the world. I made, with overtime, $50, $60 a week. I don't know what to do with all that money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother's oldest brother, who gave us our affidavit to come here, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he gave me good advice, and he helped me. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very fortunate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Life had been good to me here. And what can you say about America? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's great.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I enjoyed it, and I was determined not to live in the past. I made lots of friends here. And I lived in Brooklyn. I lived two blocks from Ebbets Field. I became a baseball fan. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really enjoyed my early years here. Then I dated till I met my wife. We've been married 41 years now. And I think that really contributed a lot to bring back and restore trust and love again into your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- looking back, it wasn't the easiest period. I think I had difficulties because it's-- it's hard to-- how can you explain it? To-- to find trust in somebody, you're skeptical, everything is questioned. You know, you have such an experience. Is this is really true or is it like the rest of it was? You-- you-- you have-- I had problems with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, of course, you realize you can't continue that way. There has to be a way. And I think I overcame this. I once-- I went to school at night, and once you know the language and you become a little bit Americanized, I think things come back to normal again. Although I was brought up Orthodox, I couldn't get back to religion. It's the thing I-- I just-- I just couldn't bring myself to believe again. But you know, the formula that God could have seen this and could let it happen. It just didn't penetrate. I just couldn't. I tried, but it didn't work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it took me a long, long time, I would say until I had my family again, then you felt, of all those millions, why me? There had to be something there. And I think there had to be something I had to be thankful for. And I think because it was deep enough, that's what was easier to come back again. I think-- I realized that there had to be something that [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] gave back to me what I have now. And we have-- how shall I say? We lead a religious life. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went back again into normal-- normal way of-- ways of thinking. I would say, the first few years, especially at the age if I was looking back, had a tremendous hate [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for Germans, for Nazis, what they did to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was like an obsession. I mean, this was also after we were first liberated. We were still in Germany. We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, you are so, look what you did to us kind of syndrome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you learned you can't go on hating all your life, either. Who was it, Santana who said that hate is a confession of inferiority? Might be true. But then, Santana never was in a German concentration camp. And Anne Frank, she still believed in the good of humanity. She wrote that before she went to Bergen-Belsen. Had she come back, maybe she would have worded a little bit differently.

speaker: interviewee
text: But again, like I said, you can't spend the rest of your life hating. And I think-- we went back to Europe a few years ago. I took my family. They wanted to know their roots. And I went back to Germany, too. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I went back to Linnich. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I went to my grandparents' graves, to the cemeteries. And surprisingly, the younger people, I was able to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] establish a relationship to communicate easier. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And the older ones tried to avoid me, and I tried to avoid them. And of course, my question always was, what do your parents tell you when you ask them why they did this to us? What's their answer? And all of them said, whenever we ask a question related to that era, they'd say, that's in the past. We don't want to talk about it. And we don't get an answer from them. So we-- I took them to Germany. I took them our house. It's still there. I went back to Holland. And I still have very good friends in Holland, classmates. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, they made a big party when I came back. We had a good time. They're good people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would say, all-in-all, life has been good to us. We have a lot to be thankful for. I think the fact that, like I said, we have a family-- my wife, my daughters, Wendy, Michelle. We have a grandson, Zachary Moses. [CRYING] We have a lot to be thankful for. And to live in this country that gave us the opportunity, to-- give us a chance to start a new life. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] You have to cry. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I think-- [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think what Shoah does, the foundation, to give us this opportunity to talk, and that there are people there who want to listen. That I think we owe them a-- a great deal of thanks. And people like my interviewer, Ms. Pat Padu, many thanks, my cameraman, America. And I think it's important that you get the opportunity to do this. I think it's important for the future generations to know what can happen when hatred [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rules and how important it is that you use the opportunity that you have when you see injustice. That you get involved, that you don't wait, and you don't stand by, and don't wait until the other person does something. You're the one that has to get involved. We have a great system. We have a great country. You have to be thankful for that. And you have to be realistic. You have to participate, and you have to make sure [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that things like this never shall happen again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I would say I thank you all for giving me the opportunity to talk about this and to tell you about it. And I think it's important that we shall never forget this. And I thank you all for your help to keep my promise that I will never forget them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of my sister, Lore, who was four years old at that time, and our nanny and me, who was one  year old at that time. And the picture was taken in Linnich. That's a picture of my mother and my sister Lore, again and me. I think I was about three or four years old at the time. And that picture was also taken in Linnich. And that's a picture of our family. And that was taken in Holland when we lived in Holland, in [? Felain. ?] And that's my father, my mother, and my sister Lore, and myself. I would say I was about 12 or 13 years old at the time. That is my Jood star. That the star that we had to wear during the German occupation in Holland. And I'd like to add, very proudly I wore it. That picture was taken in Holland, in Sittard, and I was 16 at the time. I remember it quite well. This is the certificate that I received at the city hall in Auschwitz after we were liberated. It's in several languages and it tells you that we were prisoners in Auschwitz, and we are on our way back to Holland and please give us your help, and we need it. This is my family when we were vacationing in, I think it was at Beach Haven on the shore. I think it's little Michelle and Wendy and my wife, Doris, and myself. I think this was about-- Michelle maybe was about five or six and Wendy eight. And I was much, much younger then. This is my present family as of 1996. That's my two daughters, Wendy, Michelle, my wife, Doris, and my two son-in-laws, John and Larry. This is our latest addition, our pride and joy, little Zachary Moses Martick, three months old.
